<script>

            "width": 562,
            "height": 435,
            "material_id": "xmsy8qvh",
            "borderColor": "888888",
            "playButton": false,
            "showFullscreenButton": true,

        };
        let params02 = {
            "width": 800,
            "height": 435,
            "material_id": "bf4p8vtm",
            "borderColor": "888888",
            "playButton": true,

        };
        let ggbApplet01 = new GGBApplet(params01, true);
        let ggbApplet02 = new GGBApplet(params02, true);
        window.addEventListener("load", () => {
            ggbApplet01.inject('ggb-element-01');
            ggbApplet02.inject('ggb-element-02');
        });

                    The first transformation @@math:26:1ba93b@@ is an inversion with respect to the unit circle
                    @@math:27:0f9595@@ That is, the image of a nonzero point @@math:28:cbcb38@@ is the point @@math:29:347bce@@ with the properties

                    Thus the points exterior to the circle @@math:30:a0ff10@@ are mapped onto the nonzero points
                    interior to it, and conversely. Any point on the circle is mapped onto itself.
                    The second transformation @@math:31:c12de7@@ is simply a reflection in the real axis.

An interesting property of the mapping @@math:36:24272f@@ is that it transforms
                    circles and lines into circles and lines.

You can observe this intuitively in the following applet. Things to try:
  • Select between a [[code]]Line[[/code]] or [[code]]Circle[[/code]].
  • Drag points around on the left-side window. You can also change the position of the line or circle by dragging the grey points.

Observe carefully what happens to the points @@math:37:be2cf6@@ (the image of @@math:38:304816@@ and @@math:39:49a5d8@@ respectively) on the @@math:40:8ac469@@-plane, shown on the right-side window.
  • What do you notice when the line on the @@math:41:ed829a@@-plane crosses the origin?
  • What happens when the circle on the @@math:42:5869d2@@-plane crosses the origin?

Now, using the relations

we can rewrite equation (\ref{circle01}) in the form @@math:13:4d637e@@ Since @@math:56:4a0e5d@@ equation (\ref{circle03}) becomes @@math:14:f196c7@@ and using the relations @@math:15:961552@@ we obtain @@math:16:3b2238@@ which also represents a circle or line.

It is now clear from equations (\ref{circle01}) and (\ref{circle04}) that

  2. a circle (@@math:61:83410e@@) through the origin @@math:62:79f6d3@@ in the @@math:63:866710@@ plane is transformed into a line that does not pass through the origin in the @@math:64:70fe57@@ plane;
  3. a line (@@math:65:fd7fe6@@) not passing through the origin @@math:66:f8d0c6@@ in the @@math:67:8890d3@@ plane is transformed into a circle through the origin in the @@math:68:6946f0@@ plane;
  4. a line (@@math:69:07379f@@) through the origin @@math:70:b467f9@@ in the @@math:71:511ab5@@ plane is transformed into a line through the origin in the @@math:72:64eeb4@@ plane.

Any point on the circle is mapped onto itself. The second transformation @@math:31:c12de7@@ is simply a reflection in the real axis.

Mappings by @@math:35:62caff@@

An interesting property of the mapping @@math:36:24272f@@ is that it transforms circles and lines into circles and lines.

You can observe this intuitively in the following applet. Things to try:

  • Select between a Line or Circle.
  • Drag points around on the left-side window. You can also change the position of the line or circle by dragging the grey points.

Observe carefully what happens to the points @@math:37:be2cf6@@ (the image of @@math:38:304816@@ and @@math:39:49a5d8@@ respectively) on the @@math:40:8ac469@@-plane, shown on the right-side window.

  • What do you notice when the line on the @@math:41:ed829a@@-plane crosses the origin?
  • What happens when the circle on the @@math:42:5869d2@@-plane crosses the origin?
